items.question_text: Under 6-5-2, GPS-equipped aircraft tracking a waypoint receive 'to' data rather than 'from' data. What operational adjustment does the paragraph require for crossing restrictions?
items.explanation: 6-5-2's note states that due to GPS equipment providing tracking 'to' data rather than 'from' the last waypoint, issue crossing restrictions in reference to the NEXT waypoint.

choices.text: Issue crossing restrictions in reference to the next waypoint rather than the last waypoint.
Cancel all crossing restrictions because GPS aircraft cannot comply with them.
Issue crossing restrictions only for VOR-based navigation, not GPS-equipped aircraft.
Issue the same crossing restrictions with no change in reference point.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-2, which divergence distance table must be used for DME applications above 3,000 feet above NAVAID elevation?
items.explanation: 6-5-2 provides TBL 6-5-2 for DME application which compensates for DME slant-range error. TBL 6-5-1 (non-DME) may only be used for DME when the aircraft is at 3,000 feet or less above the NAVAID elevation where slant-range error is negligible.

choices.text: TBL 6-5-1 (non-DME) for all altitudes because DME is inherently non-slant affected.
TBL 6-5-2 (DME), which compensates for slant-range error at altitudes above 3,000 feet above NAVAID elevation.
Either table may be used interchangeably at any altitude.
Neither table applies; DME aircraft use the radar separation tables instead.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-2 note, for altitudes of 3,000 feet or less above the elevation of the NAVAID, DME slant-range error is negligible and the values in _____ may be used even for DME-equipped aircraft.
items.explanation: 6-5-2's altitude note allows TBL 6-5-1 (non-DME) to be used when at 3,000 feet or less above NAVAID elevation because slant-range error becomes negligible.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-3, what is the difference between the arc-to-arc minimum (subpara a) and the arc-to-other-airspace minimum (subpara b) at 35 miles or less from the NAVAID?
items.explanation: 6-5-3a1 requires 10 miles between different arcs about the same NAVAID at ≤35 miles. 6-5-3b1 requires only 5 miles between an arc and other airspace to be protected at ≤35 miles. The arc-to-arc minimum is higher because both aircraft are on DME arcs.

choices.text: No difference; both use the same minimum at all distances.
Arc-to-arc requires 10 miles; arc-to-other-airspace requires only 5 miles (at ≤35 miles from NAVAID).
Arc-to-arc requires 5 miles; arc-to-other-airspace requires 10 miles.
Arc-to-arc applies only above FL180; arc-to-other-airspace applies below FL180.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-4a1, for direct courses via NAVAIDs or radials at FL600 and below, the protected airspace expands from 4 miles each side at 51 miles from the NAVAID to a width of _____ miles on each side at a distance of 130 miles from the NAVAID, increasing on what angle?
items.explanation: 6-5-4a1 specifies expansion on a 4-1/2 degree angle from 4 miles each side at 51 NM to 10 miles each side at 130 NM.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-4b, when a course change is 16 through 90 degrees, what overflown-side protection applies at FL180 to FL230 inclusive?
items.explanation: 6-5-4b2 requires 14 miles for the FL180-FL230 band when the course change is 16-90 degrees.

choices.text: 14 miles.
17 miles.
28 miles.
34 miles.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-4c, when a course change is 91 through 180 degrees at FL180 to FL230 inclusive, what overflown-side protection applies?
items.explanation: 6-5-4c2 requires 28 miles for the FL180-FL230 band when the course change is 91-180 degrees. This is exactly double the 14 miles required for the 16-90 degree case in the same altitude band.

choices.text: 14 miles, same as the 16-90 degree case.
28 miles.
34 miles.
17 miles, the same as above FL230 for the smaller course change.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-4, after a course change of 16-180 degrees has been completed and the aircraft is back on course, which minima apply?
items.explanation: 6-5-4d specifies that after the course changes in subparagraphs b or c have been completed and the aircraft is back on course, the appropriate minima in subparagraph a may be used — essentially, the direct-course protection resumes.

choices.text: The overflown-side protection from subparagraphs b or c continues indefinitely along the remainder of the route.
The appropriate minima in subparagraph a (direct course) may be used once the turn is complete and the aircraft is back on course.
A new 51-mile protection zone begins from the point of course change regardless of distance to the next NAVAID.
No protection is needed after the course change because the aircraft is already established on the new course.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-4b3 and c3, when a course change occurs above FL230 to FL600, what overflown-side protection applies for a 16-90 degree change vs a 91-180 degree change?
items.explanation: 6-5-4b3 gives 17 miles for 16-90 degrees above FL230; 6-5-4c3 gives 34 miles for 91-180 degrees above FL230. Note the exact doubling: 14→28 in FL180-230, 17→34 above FL230.

choices.text: 17 miles for 16-90 degrees; 34 miles for 91-180 degrees.
14 miles for 16-90 degrees; 28 miles for 91-180 degrees.
Both use 17 miles regardless of the turn angle above FL230.
28 miles for all turns above FL230.

items.question_text: Under 6-5-2, what does TBL 6-5-2 provide that TBL 6-5-1 does not?

choices.text: Higher distance values that compensate for DME slant-range error, and altitude-dependent columns (Below FL180 and FL180-FL450).
Lower distance values for non-DME aircraft only.
The same values as TBL 6-5-1 but formatted differently for radar controllers.
Values for divergence angles below 15 degrees that TBL 6-5-1 does not address.

items.explanation: TBL 6-5-2 compensates for DME slant-range error with higher values, and splits distances by altitude (Below FL180 vs FL180-FL450). TBL 6-5-1 is for non-DME only and has a single distance column.

items.situation: An aircraft on a non-established route makes a course change of 45 degrees at FL190. You need to protect the overflown side airspace.
items.question_text: Under 6-5-4b, what overflown-side protection distance applies?

choices.text: 14 miles; 45 degrees falls in the 16-90 degree range, and FL190 is in the FL180-FL230 band.
17 miles; all course changes above FL180 use the same protection.
28 miles; 45 degrees is treated as a major direction reversal above FL180.
Same as subpara a1; course changes of less than 90 degrees use direct-course protection.

items.explanation: 6-5-4B classifies 16-90 degree changes separately from 91-180. 45 degrees is in the 16-90 range. At FL190 (FL180-FL230 inclusive), subpara b2 applies with 14 miles.
items.para_context: MINIMA ALONG OTHER THAN ESTABLISHED AIRWAYS OR ROUTES

items.situation: An aircraft on a non-established route makes a 120-degree course change at FL350.

choices.text: 34 miles; 120 degrees falls in the 91-180 degree range, and FL350 is above FL230 (subpara c3 applies).
17 miles; all course changes above FL230 default to the smaller protection value.
28 miles; FL350 falls in the FL180-FL230 band for this type of turn.
The aircraft must be cleared to an established airway because turns above 90 degrees on non-established routes are not permitted above FL230.

items.explanation: 6-5-4C3 requires 34 miles for 91-180 degree course changes above FL230 to FL600. 120 degrees is in the 91-180 range.
items.para_context: MINIMA ALONG OTHER THAN ESTABLISHED AIRWAYS OR ROUTES
